title: Dynamic Web Programming - Quiz 3 (Merged)
source: Merged from uploaded Quiz 3 documents and screenshots

questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.text: JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read
JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser
JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data
Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch

questions.explanationMd: JSON can be deserialized directly into structured data, avoiding manual delimiter parsing.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "fulfilled" state of a Promise?

options.text: The Promise is waiting for a `.then()` handler to be attached
The operation encountered a network error
The Promise has been created but not yet started
The asynchronous operation completed successfully

questions.explanationMd: A fulfilled Promise has completed successfully and produced a result.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API in a web browser?

options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into strings
To define callback functions for user interface events
To manipulate the DOM after a page has loaded
To load external resources such as text and JSON asynchronously

questions.explanationMd: Fetch is used to request resources asynchronously, such as text, JSON, or other network content.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?

options.text: JSON.parse()
JSON.stringify()
JSON.encode()
JSON.deserialize()

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` converts a JavaScript value into a JSON string.

questions.questionMd: What is the key difference between `navigator.getUserMedia` and `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia`?

options.text: The newer version requires HTTPS while the deprecated version does not
The deprecated version uses Promises; the newer version uses callbacks
Only the deprecated version supports video streaming
The newer version returns a Promise; the deprecated version uses callbacks

questions.explanationMd: The modern API is Promise-based; the legacy API used callbacks.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "deserialize" a string?

options.text: To encode a string using HTML entities
To split a string into an array using a separator
To turn a serialized string back into a JavaScript object
To convert a JavaScript object into a JSON string

questions.explanationMd: Deserialization means reconstructing structured data from its serialized string form.

questions.questionMd: In a chained Promise pattern, what must `onResponse` return for the next `.then()` in the chain to receive the text data?

options.text: The response object itself
The Promise returned by `response.text()`
A JSON object created with `JSON.parse()`
A resolved string from `response.status`

questions.explanationMd: Returning `response.text()` passes a Promise that resolves with the response body text to the next handler.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct syntax for using the real Fetch API with success and error handlers?

options.text: fetch('file.txt', onSuccess, onError);
fetch('file.txt').catch(onSuccess).then(onError);
fetch('file.txt').then(onSuccess, onError);
fetch('file.txt', { success: onSuccess, error: onError });

questions.explanationMd: Fetch returns a Promise, so success and failure handlers are attached with `.then(success, error)` or `.catch(...)`.

questions.questionMd: When chaining `.then()` calls on a fetch Promise, what does the second `.then()` receive as its argument?

options.text: The value returned or resolved by the first `.then()` handler
The HTTP status code from the first `.then()`
The original fetch response object
A new fetch Promise targeting the same resource

questions.explanationMd: Each `.then()` receives the resolved value returned from the previous step in the chain.

questions.questionMd: What does `JSON.parse()` accept as its argument?

options.text: A JavaScript object to be serialized
A JSON-formatted string
A Promise returned by `fetch()`
A fetch response object

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.parse()` expects a JSON string and converts it into a JavaScript value.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly rewrites `fetch('f.txt').then(onResponse)` using an intermediate variable?

options.text: let p = onResponse; p.fetch('f.txt');
const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.resolve(onResponse);
const p = new Promise('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);
const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

questions.explanationMd: Since `fetch()` returns a Promise, it can be assigned to a variable and then chained with `.then()`.

questions.questionMd: What does JSON stand for?

options.text: Java Serialized Object Network
JavaScript Syntax Object Notation
JavaScript Object Notation
JavaScript Ordered Node

questions.explanationMd: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced for loading browser resources?

options.text: XMLHttpRequest (XHR)
JSON.parse()
loadFromFile()
navigator.mediaDevices

questions.explanationMd: Fetch modernized browser resource loading that was previously often done with XMLHttpRequest.

questions.questionMd: Why does calling `fetch()` on a local file opened via `file://` protocol result in an error?

options.text: Loading files via the `file://` protocol is disallowed by the browser
Local files must be fetched using XMLHttpRequest instead
The fetch API only supports JSON files, not text files
JavaScript blocks all file I/O on the main thread

questions.explanationMd: Browsers restrict fetching local files directly from `file://` for security reasons.

questions.questionMd: Who created JSON according to the lecture?

options.text: Ryan Dahl
Douglas Crockford
Brendan Eich
Tim Berners-Lee

questions.explanationMd: Douglas Crockford popularized and specified JSON.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.text()` return?

options.text: The raw bytes of the response body as a string immediately
A Promise that resolves with the response body as a string
A JSON object parsed from the response body
The HTTP status code as a text string

questions.explanationMd: `response.text()` returns a Promise that resolves to the body text.

questions.questionMd: What does the `fetch()` function return?

options.text: A JSON-parsed JavaScript object
A Promise
An XMLHttpRequest object
The raw text content of the requested resource

questions.explanationMd: `fetch()` immediately returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, the Fetch API is made up of how many functions?

options.text: Four
Two
Three
One

questions.explanationMd: In the lecture framing, the core API is presented as one main function: `fetch()`.

questions.questionMd: A Promise object can be in one of three states. Which of the following is NOT one of those states?

options.text: Pending
Resolved
Rejected
Fulfilled

questions.explanationMd: The standard states are pending, fulfilled, and rejected. “Resolved” is not one of the three state names here.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "pending" state of a Promise?

options.text: The state where `.then()` has been called but not yet attached
The initial state where the operation has not yet fulfilled or rejected
The state where the error handler has been triggered
The state where the operation completed but returned no data

questions.explanationMd: A Promise is pending before it settles as fulfilled or rejected.

questions.questionMd: What type of value does `JSON.stringify(object)` return?

options.text: A boolean indicating whether serialization succeeded
A Promise that resolves with the serialized data
A new JavaScript object with the same properties
A string representing the object in JSON format

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` returns a string.

questions.questionMd: How can a developer serve local files over HTTP so that `fetch()` works correctly?

options.text: Use the `file://` protocol with a special fetch configuration option
Run a local HTTP server, such as with `python -m SimpleHTTPServer`
Enable CORS headers directly in the HTML file
Switch from `fetch()` to XMLHttpRequest for local files

questions.explanationMd: Serving files over HTTP avoids the browser restrictions associated with `file://`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, how do you attach handlers to a Promise object?

options.text: Via the `.then()` method
Via the `.attach()` method
By assigning them to the `onSuccess` and `onError` properties
By passing them as arguments directly to `fetch()`

questions.explanationMd: Handlers are attached to Promises with `.then()` and optionally `.catch()`.

questions.questionMd: A Promise has a `.then()` method that accepts which of the following?

options.text: A timeout value and a retry count
A single callback that handles both success and failure identically
Functions to execute on success or on error
A URL string and an HTTP method

questions.explanationMd: `.then()` accepts callback functions for fulfillment and optionally rejection handling.

questions.questionMd: In the lecture's chained fetch example, what is the correct order of asynchronous operations?

options.text: `fetch` completes, then `onResponse` runs, then `response.text()` completes, then `onStreamProcessed` runs
`onStreamProcessed` runs first, then `fetch` completes, then `onResponse` runs
`fetch` and `response.text()` run simultaneously before any handlers fire
`response.text()` runs before `fetch` completes

questions.explanationMd: The response arrives first, then the first handler runs, then the body text is resolved, then the next handler runs.

questions.questionMd: Which method on a fetch `response` object provides built-in support for parsing JSON directly?

options.text: `response.parse()`
`response.decode()`
`response.json()`
`response.text()`

questions.explanationMd: `response.json()` reads the body and parses it as JSON.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.status` contain when used inside a fetch success handler?

options.text: The name of the file that was fetched
A boolean indicating whether the request succeeded
The HTTP status code of the request, such as 200
The text content of the fetched resource

questions.explanationMd: `response.status` holds the numeric HTTP status code.

questions.questionMd: When using `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia()` with a Promise, how is the camera stream accessed?

options.text: By reading the return value of the function directly
By listening for a DOM event fired when the camera opens
By passing an `onSuccess` callback as the second argument
By attaching a `.then()` handler that receives the stream as its argument

questions.explanationMd: The Promise resolves with the media stream, which is received in a `.then()` callback.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "serialize" an object?

options.text: To turn an object into a string that can later be deserialized
To load an object from an external file using fetch
To send an object directly to a server over HTTP
To convert a string into a JavaScript object

questions.explanationMd: Serialization means converting structured data into a storable or transferable string form.

questions.questionMd: In the completed fetch example, which JavaScript method is used to break the fetched text into individual image URLs?

options.text: `text.join(' ')`
`text.replace(' ', \,)`
`text.slice(' ')`
`text.split(' ')`

questions.explanationMd: The example uses `split` to separate the fetched text into multiple URLs.

questions.questionMd: Which command is used to serve local files over HTTP so that `fetch()` can access them during development?

options.text: `python3 -m http.server`
`python3 -m fetch-server`
`npm start http`
`node server.js`

questions.explanationMd: A simple local HTTP server such as `python3 -m http.server` is enough for development.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement best captures the relationship between using and defining Promise?

options.text: Promises are easier to define than to use
Promises must be fully understood internally before they can be used
Promises are easier to use than to define
Promises are equally straightforward to both define and use

questions.explanationMd: Using existing Promises is generally simpler than implementing Promise-producing logic yourself.

questions.questionMd: In the discography page example, how does the `SortButton` class communicate a sort action back to the `App` class?

options.text: It directly modifies the `albumInfo` property of the `App` instance
It dispatches a custom keyboard event that triggers the App's event listener
It fires a built-in DOM click event that the `App` class listens for on the document
It calls an `onClickCallback` function passed to it during construction, supplying the sort function as an argument

questions.explanationMd: The component communicates upward by invoking a callback it received from its parent.

questions.questionMd: When defining methods inside an ES6 class body, which keyword must you omit?

options.text: class
function
const
return

questions.explanationMd: Methods are declared directly inside a class body without the `function` keyword.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what is an important limitation of the `CustomEvent` API in JavaScript?

options.text: CustomEvents are not supported in any browser that uses the V8 engine
CustomEvents can only be dispatched and listened to on HTML elements, not on arbitrary class instances
CustomEvents can only be created inside ES6 class constructors
CustomEvents cannot carry any additional data in their event object

questions.explanationMd: The lecture highlights DOM-centric event dispatch/listening, not arbitrary class-instance event systems.

questions.questionMd: What problem does calling `this.methodName = this.methodName.bind(this)` in a constructor solve?

options.text: It prevents the method from being called more than once per event
It ensures `this` always refers to the class instance when the method is used as an event handler
It registers the method as a global function accessible from any scope
It copies the method's code into the HTML element for faster execution

questions.explanationMd: Binding preserves the intended instance context when the method is passed as a callback.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is a characteristic of first-class functions in JavaScript?

options.text: Functions must always be declared before the class definition
Functions cannot be stored in variables or object properties
Functions require the `function` keyword and cannot use arrow syntax
Functions can be passed as parameters to other functions

questions.explanationMd: First-class functions can be stored, passed, and returned like other values.

questions.questionMd: Given the JSON object `{"albums": [{"name": "Daydream", "year": 1995}]}` parsed into a variable called `data`, how would you access the year of the first album?

options.text: `data[0].albums.year`
`data.albums.year[0]`
`data.albums[0].year`
`data["year"]["albums"][0]`

questions.explanationMd: The array is under `albums`, so the first item's `year` is `data.albums[0].year`.

questions.questionMd: When passing a callback to `list.findIndex(callback)`, what value should the callback return when the desired element is found?

options.text: The index of the element
`null`
`true`
The element itself

questions.explanationMd: `findIndex` expects a predicate callback that returns a truthy value for a match.

questions.questionMd: How does `response.text()` read the response data according to the lecture?

options.text: Synchronously, by returning the string directly
Asynchronously, by returning a Promise that resolves with the response string
By dispatching a DOM event when reading is complete
By writing the result into a global variable

questions.explanationMd: `response.text()` is asynchronous and resolves later with the body text.

questions.questionMd: Which set of programming languages is associated with front-end web development?

options.text: SQL, XML, and SGML
HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
Node.js, PHP, and Go
Python, Java, and Ruby

questions.explanationMd: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript are the core front-end web technologies.

questions.questionMd: In JavaScript, what is a closure?

options.text: A function declared inside another function that retains access to the outer function's variables
A method that prevents other code from modifying an object's properties
A special syntax for defining private class fields in ES6
A technique for combining multiple event listeners into a single handler

questions.explanationMd: A closure preserves access to variables from its lexical scope.

questions.questionMd: In the TCP/IP model, what is the responsibility of the Internet Protocol (IP)?

options.text: To convert hostnames into human-readable domain names
To encrypt data before transmission over the network
To route TCP packets to the correct destination address
To guarantee that all packets arrive in the correct order

questions.explanationMd: IP is responsible for addressing and routing packets to the destination network/interface.

questions.questionMd: How does the lecture describe XMLHttpRequest (XHR) compared to the Fetch API?

options.text: Simpler but less flexible
More secure and widely supported
Faster and more reliable
Clunky and harder to use

questions.explanationMd: The lecture characterizes XHR as a more awkward older API compared with Fetch.

questions.questionMd: Which MIME type indicates that the content being transferred is an HTML web page?

options.text: application/json
text/plain
text/html
image/jpeg

questions.explanationMd: `text/html` is the standard MIME type for HTML documents.

questions.questionMd: Inside an ES6 class constructor, what does the `this` keyword refer to?

options.text: The HTML element that triggered the event
The new object instance being created
The parent class from which the current class inherits
The global window object of the browser

questions.explanationMd: Inside a constructor, `this` refers to the instance being initialized.

questions.questionMd: What is the role of SGML (Standard Generalized Markup Language) in relation to HTML?

options.text: SGML is a transport protocol that HTTP was built upon
SGML is a standard for specifying markup languages and was used by Tim Berners-Lee to create the HTML specification
SGML is a scripting language that adds interactivity to static HTML pages
SGML is a stylesheet language used to define the visual presentation of XML documents

questions.explanationMd: HTML was historically defined as an application of SGML.

questions.questionMd: In the hypothetical asynchronous version of `loadFromFile()` shown in the lecture, how are success and failure handled?

options.text: By checking a return value after the function completes
By wrapping the call in a try-catch block
By polling the function repeatedly until it resolves
By passing `onSuccess` and `onFail` as callback function arguments

questions.explanationMd: The asynchronous pattern presented uses explicit success/failure callbacks.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced for loading browser resources?

options.text: navigator.mediaDevices
XMLHttpRequest (XHR)
JSON.parse()
loadFromFile()

questions.explanationMd: Fetch replaced the older XMLHttpRequest pattern for many browser network tasks.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JSON string back into a JavaScript object?

options.text: JSON.decode()
JSON.stringify()
JSON.parse()
JSON.hydrate()

questions.explanationMd: JSON strings are parsed into JavaScript values with `JSON.parse()`.

questions.questionMd: Which method on a fetch `response` object provides built-in support for parsing JSON directly?

options.text: `response.text()`
`response.json()`
`response.parse()`
`response.decode()`

questions.explanationMd: The built-in JSON-parsing method is `response.json()`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement about `.then()` is correct?

options.text: It can only be called once per Promise object
It replaces the need for the `fetch()` function
It immediately executes the fetch request synchronously
It lets you attach functions to execute when a Promise succeeds or fails

questions.explanationMd: `.then()` registers callbacks to run when a Promise settles.

questions.questionMd: What does `JSON.parse()` accept as its argument?

options.text: A Promise returned by `fetch()`
A JavaScript object to be serialized
A fetch response object
A JSON-formatted string

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.parse()` parses a JSON string.

questions.questionMd: What does `fetch()` return?

options.text: The raw text content of the requested resource
An XMLHttpRequest object
A JSON-parsed JavaScript object
A Promise

questions.explanationMd: `fetch()` returns a Promise.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?

options.text: JSON.stringify()
JSON.parse()
JSON.deserialize()
JSON.encode()

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` serializes a JavaScript object to JSON text.

questions.questionMd: How does `response.text()` read the response data according to the lecture?

options.text: Synchronously, by returning the string directly
By dispatching a DOM event when reading is complete
Asynchronously, by returning a Promise that resolves with the response string
By writing the result into a global variable

questions.explanationMd: `response.text()` resolves asynchronously with the body text.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct syntax for using the real Fetch API with success and error handlers?

options.text: fetch('file.txt').catch(onSuccess).then(onError);
fetch('file.txt').then(onSuccess, onError);
fetch('file.txt', { success: onSuccess, error: onError });
fetch('file.txt', onSuccess, onError);

questions.explanationMd: The lecture's Promise-based pattern attaches success/error handlers with `.then(success, error)`.
